title: The Remedy Devised
subtitle: Mercy and Truth contend before God for the ruined race of Adam
gospelRef: none (Vita Christi, Part I, ch. II — pre-Incarnation; no Gospel quoted)

stillness: Be still, and weigh the long ages.
Five thousand years and nearly two hundred more,
mankind lay wretched, and could not rise.
gospel: This chapter quotes no Gospel; it tells the counsel of God before the Word was made flesh.
It is written: God, the Father of mercies and the God of all consolation,
looked with clemency upon the state of our damnation,
and resolved to set us free by Himself.
He gave us a sign by the olive branch the dove bore to those shut in the ark,
which held forth the mercy of God to come for those shut in the dark.
behold: Consider how, in the beginning, Lucifer was created,
and raised himself against God his Creator,
and in the twinkling of an eye was cast down from the height of heaven into hell.
Behold the envious deceiver entering the serpent,
speaking words of deceit through its mouth to the woman,
and, deceiving her, bringing death upon the whole human race.
See us all bound to enter the prison of hell,
from which the help of no mere man could tear us free.
Behold the blessed spirits, pitying our ruin,
beseeching the Lord more urgently as the fullness of time drew near.
And see, before God, the great debate:
Mercy, holding peace at her side,
and between them a mighty controversy joined.
consider: Mercy said to God: the rational creature has need of pity,
for she is made wretched, and exceedingly miserable;
the time has come, then, to have mercy on her.
But Truth answered: I beg, O Lord, let the word You spoke be fulfilled.
Let Adam wholly die, with all who were in him,
since by transgressing he tasted the forbidden fruit.
Mercy cried: Why, O Lord, have You made me?
Truth herself knows that I perish, if You never show mercy.
And Truth: if the transgressor escapes Your sentence,
Your truth has perished, and does not abide forever.
It seemed there was no way that, around man,
mercy and truth could be preserved together.
Then the King wrote His sentence:
Let a good death be found, and both have what they ask —
that Adam die, and yet obtain mercy.
Let one be found who dies out of love, not owing death;
so death cannot hold the innocent, but He will make in it an opening,
through which the freed may pass.
Woe to us, wretched ones of this present age,
who are not moved toward the grace given us
as the men of old were moved toward the grace promised.
I am pierced and confounded within myself, says Bernard,
and scarcely now hold back my tears,
so ashamed am I of the lukewarmness and torpor of these miserable times.
prayer: I beseech You, O Lord, let the word You have spoken be fulfilled.
Why, O Lord, have You made me?
For Truth herself knows that I perish, if You never show mercy.
The rational creature has need of Your pity,
for she is made wretched, and exceedingly miserable.
The time has come, O Lord, to have mercy upon her.

latinJewel.latin: Inveniatur ergo qui ex charitate moriatur, non obnoxius morti; et sic mors non poterit tenere innoxium, sed faciet in ea foramen, per quod transeant liberati.
latinJewel.english: Let one be found, then, who dies out of love, not owing death; and so death cannot hold the innocent one, but He will make in it an opening, through which the freed may pass.
